data.title: PAMT (PAMT) Losses Deepen To US$2.48 TTM EPS Loss Challenging Bullish Narratives
data.description:
PAMT (PAMT) has just opened Q1 2026 with a trailing twelve month revenue base of about US$598.1 million and a basic EPS loss of US$2.48 per share, setting the stage for another result where the top line is sizeable but profitability remains under pressure. Over recent quarters the company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$182.6 million in Q3 2024 to US$141.3 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly basic EPS shifted from a profit of US$0.11 to a loss of US$1.40. The latest update therefore lands against a backdrop of heavier losses and squeezed margins. With the share price at US$9.71, investors are likely to focus on how quickly margins can stabilize and whether revenue quality can support a path toward more sustainable results.

See our full analysis for PAMT.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the widely followed narratives around PAMT's growth potential, risk profile, and long term profitability story.

US$598.1m sales, but losses deepening on trailing basis

  • Over the last twelve months, PAMT generated about US$598.1 million in revenue but recorded a net loss of US$52.6 million, with basic EPS of a US$2.48 loss.
  • What stands out for a cautiously bearish view is that management’s reported losses have grown at about 58.5% per year over the past five years,
    • bears highlight that this multi year loss trend sits alongside sizeable annual revenue in the US$598 million to US$714.6 million range, which points to pressure on profitability rather than a lack of sales,
    • critics also point to the Q4 2025 net loss of US$29.3 million on US$141.3 million of revenue as an example of how quarterly performance can still translate sizable sales into sizeable losses.

Quarterly swing from Q3 2024 profit to steady losses

  • After a Q3 2024 profit of US$2.4 million on US$182.6 million of revenue with basic EPS of US$0.11, subsequent quarters show losses, including Q4 2024 net income of a US$31.6 million loss and Q4 2025 net income of a US$29.3 million loss.
  • For a more optimistic angle, the AI narrative focuses on PAMT as an established cross border truckload and logistics operator,
    • supporters emphasize that the company’s presence across the US, Mexico, and Canada and its mix of automotive, retail, and manufactured goods freight offer a diversified demand base alongside these loss making quarters,
    • the same bullish storyline references PAMT’s logistics and brokerage services as a potentially higher margin complement to the core trucking operations, even though the current reported EPS and net income figures do not yet show that in the bottom line.

P/S of 0.3x versus 0.7x peers and 1.3x industry

  • PAMT trades at a P/S of 0.3x, compared with a peer average of 0.7x and a US Transportation industry average of 1.3x, while the company remains unprofitable on trailing figures.
  • That gap gives bears and bulls very different talking points,
    • bears argue that the low P/S multiple reflects concern about the five year loss growth rate of 58.5% and the fact that operating cash flow does not well cover debt,
    • more constructive investors instead see the same 0.3x sales level as a potential valuation discount against sizeable revenue of roughly US$598.1 million over the last twelve months.

Approach 1: Nordea Bank Abp Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently a bank turns shareholder equity into profits after covering its required cost of equity. In simple terms, it asks whether each euro invested in Nordea Bank Abp is expected to earn more than investors require for the risk they are taking.

For Nordea Bank Abp, the starting Book Value is €8.86 per share, with a Stable Book Value estimate of €10.46 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 4 analysts. Stable EPS is €1.60 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from 16 analysts. The Average Return on Equity used in the model is 15.30%.

The model applies a Cost of Equity of €0.71 per share. After covering that cost, the expected Excess Return is €0.89 per share, which is the key input for estimating long term value creation.

Bringing these elements together, the Excess Returns valuation implies an intrinsic value of €29.44 per share. Compared with the current share price of €15.99, this suggests a 45.7% discount, meaning that the stock screens as significantly undervalued on this approach.

P/E is a useful yardstick for profitable companies because it links what you pay directly to the earnings each share generates. In general, higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tend to justify a lower one.

Nordea Bank Abp currently trades on a P/E of 11.53x. That sits above the Banks industry average of 10.89x and above the peer average of 9.56x, so on simple comparisons the shares look slightly more expensively rated than many peers.

data.description:

  • The stock has had a mixed short term patch, with a 2.9% decline over the last week, a 4.5% gain over the last 30 days, a 6.4% year to date decline, and a 69.0% return over the last year, alongside a 135.1% return over three years and 33.4% over five years.

Approach 1: General Motors Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes the cash General Motors is expected to generate in the future and then discounts those projected cash flows back into today’s dollars to estimate what the entire business might be worth right now.

For General Motors, the latest twelve-month free cash flow sits at about $13.6b. The current DCF model uses a 2-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, with analyst projections feeding into the earlier years and Simply Wall St extrapolating further out. For example, projected free cash flow is $10.2b in 2026, $12.1b in 2027, and $12.2b in 2028, with estimates then extended out to 2035, where projected free cash flow is $14.0b.

After discounting all of those projected cash flows back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $132.21 per share. Against a recent share price of roughly $75.77, that implies the stock is about 42.7% undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for General Motors.

Approach 2: General Motors Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are currently paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties directly to what you care about as a shareholder, the earnings that can eventually be paid out or reinvested.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple.

General Motors currently trades on a P/E of 28.1x. That sits above the Auto industry average of 18.7x and below the peer average of 55.6x. Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for General Motors is 31.7x, which is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that might be reasonable given the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors.

This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison against peers or the industry, because it adjusts for company specific traits rather than assuming all Auto names deserve the same multiple. With the actual P/E of 28.1x below the Fair Ratio of 31.7x, General Motors screens as undervalued on this earnings multiple view.
